news_summary: Advancements in laser technology are enabling rapid metal transformation, while innovative efforts are expanding internet access in Iran despite restrictions.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès de la technologie laser permettent une transformation rapide des métaux, tandis que des initiatives innovantes élargissent l'accès à Internet en Iran malgré les restrictions.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la tecnología láser están permitiendo una rápida transformación de los metales, mientras que las iniciativas innovadoras están ampliando el acceso a Internet en Irán a pesar de las restricciones.

articles.title: This laser turns metal into a star-like plasma in trillionths of a second
articles.summary: In a striking glimpse into extreme physics, scientists have captured the split-second chaos that unfolds when powerful laser flashes blast matter into a superheated plasma. By combining two cutting-edge lasers, researchers were able to track how copper atoms lose and regain electrons in trillionths of a second, creating and dissolving highly charged ions in a rapid, almost cinematic sequence.
articles.content:
When intense laser flashes strike matter, they can knock electrons out of their positions around atomic nuclei. This process creates plasma, an extremely hot state made up of charged particles known as ions and electrons. Researchers at Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) have now captured this ionization process with unprecedented detail, as reported in Nature Communications.

To achieve this, the team combined two advanced laser systems: an X-ray free-electron laser and the high-intensity optical laser ReLaX. Both were used at the HED-HiBEF experimental station at the European XFEL in Schenefeld near Hamburg. Their work provides new insight into how high-energy lasers interact with matter under extreme conditions. It also introduces a promising method for improving diagnostics in laser fusion research.

Tracking Ionization in Trillionths of a Second

Ionization unfolds incredibly fast, within picoseconds, or just a few trillionths of a second. Capturing such rapid changes requires even shorter laser pulses.

"These are exactly the conditions provided by the two lasers that have pulse durations of just 25 and 30 femtoseconds -- that is, trillionths of a second," explains Dr. Lingen Huang, head of experimentation in HZDR's Division of High-Energy Density.

With these ultrashort pulses, researchers could observe how plasma forms and evolves almost in real time.

Turning a Copper Wire Into Superhot Plasma

The experiment begins with an intense burst of light striking a very thin copper wire, about one-seventh the thickness of a human hair. The energy delivered is immense, reaching about 250 trillion megawatts per square centimeter over a tiny area for an extremely brief moment. Such conditions are usually found only in extreme cosmic environments, such as near neutron stars or during gamma-ray bursts.

The copper wire instantly vaporizes, producing plasma with temperatures of several million degrees. As this happens, copper atoms lose multiple electrons and become highly ionized.

Researchers then use a second laser pulse, called the probe pulse, to examine the plasma. This pulse, generated by the European XFEL, emits an intense flash of hard X-rays. By recording how these X-rays interact with the plasma, scientists can capture a sequence of snapshots, similar to frames in a movie. This pump-probe approach allows them to follow the plasma's evolution step by step.

Measuring Highly Charged Copper Ions

The X-ray pulses are carefully tuned to interact with Cu²²⁺ ions, copper atoms that have lost 22 electrons. The photon energy of 8.2 kiloelectronvolts matches a specific electronic transition in these ions, a process known as resonant absorption.

After absorbing the X-rays, the ions emit their own distinctive X-ray radiation.

"In our pump-probe experiment, we exactly measure the temporal development of this stimulated X-ray emission," says Huang. "Because it shows us how many Cu22+ ions are present in the plasma at any given time."

A Precise Timeline of Plasma Evolution

The measurements reveal a clear sequence of events. Right after the laser hits the wire, Cu22+ ions begin to form. Their numbers rise quickly and reach a peak after about two and a half picoseconds. After that, recombination begins, and the number of ions steadily declines. Within roughly ten picoseconds, these highly charged ions disappear completely.

"No one has ever looked at this type of ionization so precisely before," says Prof. Tom Cowan, former director of the Institute of Radiation Physics at HZDR.

Electron Waves Drive the Process

Computer simulations helped the researchers understand what drives this behavior. The initial laser pulse strips only a few electrons from the copper atoms. These electrons carry high energy and move through the material like a wave, knocking additional electrons free from neighboring atoms.

"They are so energy rich that they spread out like a wave and knock ever more electrons out of neighboring copper atoms," explains Cowan.

Over time, these electrons lose energy and are gradually recaptured by the ions. As recombination continues, the atoms return to a neutral state.

Implications for Laser Fusion Research

"This experiment demonstrates how powerful our lasers are and paves the way for future laser fusion facilities," concludes Dr. Ulf Zastrau, who is responsible for the HED-HIBEF experiment station at the European XFEL -- because laser fusion is also based on extremely hot plasmas that are heated up by lasers and the resulting electron waves.

"Thanks to our new concrete findings, we can now focus on continuing to refine our simulations of these processes," explains Zastrau. Accurate simulations are essential for designing efficient and reliable laser fusion reactors in the future.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement where researchers used advanced laser technology to capture ultrafast plasma formation and ionization processes with unprecedented precision. This breakthrough enhances understanding of extreme physics and has meaningful implications for improving laser fusion research, which could lead to cleaner energy solutions benefiting society broadly.
articles.personality_title: Scientists capture ultrafast plasma formation with powerful lasers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When very strong laser flashes hit metal, they can knock electrons away from atoms, turning the metal into plasma, a superhot state made of charged particles. This process happens incredibly fast, in just trillionths of a second, and is important for understanding extreme physics and improving laser fusion, a potential clean energy method.

**What happened** – Researchers at Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) used two advanced lasers at the European XFEL facility in Germany to watch how copper atoms lose and regain electrons in trillionths of a second. They first hit a thin copper wire with an intense laser pulse, turning it into plasma with temperatures of millions of degrees. Then, a second X-ray laser pulse took quick snapshots of the plasma as it changed.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen such detailed steps of plasma formation and ionization so clearly. They observed how copper atoms become highly charged ions and then slowly return to normal in about ten trillionths of a second. Understanding this fast process helps scientists improve computer models that guide laser fusion research, which aims to create energy by heating plasma with lasers.

**What's next step** – The team will use these new observations to make better simulations of plasma behavior during laser fusion. This will help design more efficient and reliable fusion reactors in the future, bringing us closer to new clean energy technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using two powerful lasers, scientists have captured the fastest steps of plasma creation in metal, paving the way for advances in laser fusion energy research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques capturent la formation ultrarapide du plasma avec des lasers puissants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsque des éclairs laser très puissants frappent un métal, ils peuvent arracher des électrons aux atomes, transformant le métal en plasma, un état superchauffé composé de particules chargées. Ce processus se déroule incroyablement vite, en seulement des billionièmes de seconde, et est important pour comprendre la physique extrême et améliorer la fusion laser, une méthode potentielle d’énergie propre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) ont utilisé deux lasers avancés au centre européen XFEL en Allemagne pour observer comment les atomes de cuivre perdent et retrouvent des électrons en billionièmes de seconde. Ils ont d’abord frappé un fin fil de cuivre avec une impulsion laser intense, le transformant en plasma à des millions de degrés. Puis, un second laser à rayons X a pris des clichés rapides du plasma en évolution.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques voient aussi clairement les étapes détaillées de la formation du plasma et de l’ionisation. Ils ont observé comment les atomes de cuivre deviennent des ions très chargés puis redeviennent normaux en environ dix billionièmes de seconde. Comprendre ce processus rapide aide à améliorer les modèles informatiques qui guident la recherche sur la fusion laser, qui vise à produire de l’énergie en chauffant le plasma avec des lasers.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe utilisera ces nouvelles observations pour affiner les simulations du comportement du plasma lors de la fusion laser. Cela aidera à concevoir des réacteurs de fusion plus efficaces et fiables, rapprochant ainsi les technologies d’énergie propre.

**Une phrase clé** – Grâce à deux lasers puissants, des scientifiques ont capturé les étapes les plus rapides de la création de plasma dans le métal, ouvrant la voie à des progrès dans la recherche sur l’énergie de fusion laser.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos capturan la formación ultrarrápida de plasma con potentes láseres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando destellos láser muy intensos golpean un metal, pueden arrancar electrones de los átomos, convirtiendo el metal en plasma, un estado supercaliente formado por partículas cargadas. Este proceso ocurre muy rápido, en solo billonésimas de segundo, y es importante para entender la física extrema y mejorar la fusión láser, un método posible para energía limpia.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) usaron dos láseres avanzados en la instalación europea XFEL en Alemania para observar cómo los átomos de cobre pierden y recuperan electrones en billonésimas de segundo. Primero golpearon un delgado alambre de cobre con un pulso láser intenso, convirtiéndolo en plasma a millones de grados. Luego, un segundo pulso láser de rayos X tomó rápidas fotografías del plasma mientras cambiaba.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven con tanto detalle las etapas de formación del plasma y la ionización. Observaron cómo los átomos de cobre se vuelven iones muy cargados y luego regresan a su estado normal en unos diez billonésimos de segundo. Entender este proceso rápido ayuda a mejorar los modelos computacionales que guían la investigación de fusión láser, que busca crear energía calentando plasma con láseres.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo usará estas nuevas observaciones para mejorar las simulaciones del comportamiento del plasma durante la fusión láser. Esto ayudará a diseñar reactores de fusión más eficientes y confiables, acercándonos a tecnologías de energía limpia.

**Una frase clave** – Usando dos potentes láseres, científicos capturaron los pasos más rápidos de la creación de plasma en metal, abriendo camino para avances en la investigación de energía de fusión láser.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a thin copper wire glowing intensely as it transforms into a radiant, star-like plasma with swirling waves of charged particles and bright sparks symbolizing electron waves, set against a soft, warm background of natural copper and golden hues.

articles.title: The clandestine network smuggling Starlink tech into Iran to beat internet blackout
articles.summary: Sahand tells the BBC World Service he sends satellite internet terminals into Iran to help show "the real picture".
articles.content: He says the operation he is involved with, including the purchase of the terminals, is funded by Iranians abroad and others who want to help those in the country. He says they do not receive funds from any states.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a clandestine network smuggling Starlink satellite internet terminals into Iran to help citizens bypass internet blackouts and access uncensored information. This has a positive real-world impact by promoting freedom of information and connectivity under restrictive conditions, benefiting a broad population. The story is focused and provides context about the funding and purpose of the operation.
articles.personality_title: Secret network sends Starlink internet into Iran to bypass blackouts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Iran, the government often blocks internet access to control what people can see and share online. This makes it hard for many Iranians to get true information from outside their country.

**What happened** – A secret group, supported by Iranians living abroad and other helpers, is smuggling Starlink satellite internet devices into Iran. These devices allow people to connect to the internet through satellites, avoiding local restrictions. This effort is not funded by any government but by private individuals who want to help Iranians get real news.

**Impact** – This network helps people in Iran see the world as it really is, even when the government tries to block information. It supports freedom of information and helps many people stay connected during times of internet shutdowns.

**What's next step** – The group plans to keep sending more Starlink devices into Iran to reach more people. This could help even more Iranians access uncensored internet and stay informed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A secret network is quietly bringing satellite internet to Iranians to help them bypass government blackouts and access true information.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un réseau secret envoie Internet Starlink en Iran pour contourner les coupures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Iran, le gouvernement bloque souvent l’accès à Internet pour contrôler ce que les gens peuvent voir et partager en ligne. Cela rend difficile pour de nombreux Iraniens d’obtenir des informations fiables venant de l’extérieur.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe secret, soutenu par des Iraniens vivant à l’étranger et d’autres aidants, fait entrer en Iran des appareils Starlink pour Internet par satellite. Ces appareils permettent aux gens de se connecter à Internet via des satellites, sans passer par les réseaux locaux bloqués. Cette action n’est financée par aucun gouvernement, mais par des particuliers voulant aider les Iraniens.

**Impact** – Ce réseau aide les Iraniens à voir le monde tel qu’il est vraiment, même lorsque le gouvernement bloque l’information. Il soutient la liberté d’information et permet à beaucoup de rester connectés durant les coupures d’Internet.

**Prochaine étape** – Le groupe prévoit d’envoyer encore plus d’appareils Starlink en Iran pour toucher davantage de personnes. Cela pourrait aider encore plus d’Iraniens à accéder à un Internet non censuré et à rester informés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un réseau secret apporte discrètement Internet par satellite aux Iraniens pour contourner les coupures du gouvernement et accéder à une information vraie.
articles.personality_title_es: Red secreta envía internet Starlink a Irán para evitar apagones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Irán, el gobierno a menudo bloquea el acceso a internet para controlar lo que la gente puede ver y compartir en línea. Esto dificulta que muchos iraníes obtengan información verdadera del exterior.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo secreto, apoyado por iraníes que viven en el extranjero y otros colaboradores, está enviando dispositivos de internet por satélite Starlink a Irán. Estos dispositivos permiten a las personas conectarse a internet a través de satélites, evitando las restricciones locales. Esta operación no recibe fondos de ningún gobierno, sino de particulares que quieren ayudar a los iraníes.

**Impacto** – Esta red ayuda a que la gente en Irán vea el mundo tal como es, incluso cuando el gobierno intenta bloquear la información. Apoya la libertad de información y permite que muchas personas sigan conectadas durante los cortes de internet.

**Próximo paso** – El grupo planea enviar más dispositivos Starlink a Irán para llegar a más personas. Esto podría ayudar a que aún más iraníes accedan a internet sin censura y se mantengan informados.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una red secreta está llevando internet satelital a los iraníes para ayudarles a evitar los apagones del gobierno y acceder a información verdadera.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing constellation of interconnected satellite dishes shaped like stars gently hovering over a stylized map of Iran, with soft beams of light weaving through desert landscapes and mountains symbolizing secret communication lines, all rendered in natural earth tones and muted blues.
